_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Data Types
summary: Choose a type that matches the value you need to store.
refresher: Use `int` for whole numbers, `double` for decimals, `char` for one character, `bool` for true or false, and `std::string` for text. The type affects which values and calculations are possible.

blocks.body: A type describes the kind of value a variable can store. Start with five common choices: `int` for whole numbers, `double` for decimals, `char` for one character, `bool` for true or false, and `std::string` for text.

The type matters during calculations. Dividing two integers drops the decimal part. Storing a decimal in an integer also drops the fraction. When the fraction matters, use a `double` before the calculation happens.

blocks.title: A useful Python comparison
blocks.body: Python uses `/` to produce a decimal result. In C++, `7 / 2` is `3` because both values are integers. Write `7.0 / 2` or convert one value to `double` when you need `3.5`.

blocks.caption: Changing total to a double before division keeps the fractional part, so seven divided by two becomes 3.5.
Keep fractional data in a matching type.

blocks.body: The first declaration changes `3.75` into the whole number `3`. The second keeps the decimal value. C++ may allow a conversion that loses information, so compare the variable's type with the value you actually need.

blocks.title: A type can change the answer
blocks.body: Putting a decimal into an `int` removes the fractional part. The compiler may accept the line even when the result is not what the problem asks for.

blocks.why: The char type represents one character value such as a letter.
Conversion to int truncates the fractional part rather than rounding it.
